questions.question: Which API style is generally best for high-volume communication between your own internal microservices?
options: REST with JSON, for human readability
gRPC, for compact binary payloads, strong typing, and HTTP/2 performance
GraphQL, because internal services need flexible queries
It doesn't matter; all three perform identically
questions.explanation: gRPC uses Protocol Buffers (compact binary) over HTTP/2, giving small payloads and fast parsing — ideal for service-to-service traffic where both ends are yours and volume is high. REST's JSON and GraphQL's query engine add overhead you don't need internally.

questions.question: What is 'over-fetching' in REST?
options: Making too many requests per second
Receiving more fields than you need because the endpoint returns a fixed shape
Fetching data before the user requests it
Requesting data that doesn't exist yet
questions.explanation: Over-fetching means the endpoint returns its full fixed response (e.g. the whole user object) even when the client only needed one field like the name. The wasted bytes add up, especially on mobile networks. GraphQL eliminates this by letting clients pick fields.

questions.question: How does GraphQL solve both over-fetching and under-fetching?
options: By compressing all responses with gzip
The client specifies exactly the fields it wants and gets them in a single round trip
By caching every possible query in advance
By forbidding nested data
questions.explanation: With GraphQL the client declares the precise shape it needs (e.g. name + last 3 post titles), so no extra fields come back (no over-fetch) and related data is assembled in one request (no under-fetch / multiple round trips).

questions.question: Why is Protocol Buffers (used by gRPC) more efficient than JSON?
options: It encrypts data, making it smaller
It is a compact binary format with a known schema, so it's smaller on the wire and faster to parse than text JSON
It only works for integers
It avoids using HTTP entirely
questions.explanation: Protobuf serializes to a compact binary representation using a predefined schema, so field names aren't repeated as text and values are packed tightly. That means fewer bytes transmitted and much faster parsing than parsing textual JSON.

questions.question: A large company uses GraphQL at the edge for its mobile app and gRPC between backend services. What does this illustrate?
options: The company hasn't standardized and should pick one
The styles coexist by design — each is chosen for the relationship it fits best
GraphQL is being phased out
gRPC is only a temporary measure
questions.explanation: The styles are not ranked; they coexist. GraphQL fits a flexible, latency-sensitive client (mobile), while gRPC fits high-volume internal service-to-service calls. The right question is always 'which fits this relationship?', not 'which is best overall?'.
